centerText: President Donald Trump has paid tribute to Victor Willis, the lead singer of the 1970s disco group Village People, who has died at the age of 74. "He was a great and happy guy who loved that I used his group's song, YMCA, at my Rallies. It became a "monster" hit, again, 30 years after its original launch," the president wrote in a post on Truth Social. In fact, "Y.M.C.A." was released in 1978, and quickly became a fan favorite. In recent years, the song was played regularly at Trump's rallies. Read More on News The Village People confirmed Willis’ death in a post to social media, where they wrote: "We are profoundly sad to announce the death of Victor Willis, lead singer of Village People." Willis, who was born in Texas, co-wrote some of the band's biggest hits, including "Go West," "In The Navy" and "Y.M.C.A." ... Willis initially requested that Trump stop using the song, but he later changed his stance and the group performed at a pre-inauguration rally ahead of Trump's second term in the White House. In a statement shared to Facebook in December 2024, Willis wrote: "Y.M.C.A. has benefited greatly from use by the President Elect. For example, Y.M.C.A. was stuck at #2 on the Billboard chart prior to the President Elect’s use. However, the song finally made it to #1 on a Billboard chart after over 45 years (and held on to #1 for two weeks) due to the President Elect’s use." Paying tribute to Willis, the president wrote: "We will think of Victor every time YMCA is played, like today, and all throughout this July Fourth Birthday week. My condolences to his wonderful family and group, Victor Willis will be sorely missed, God Bless Him!!! Village People Confirm Death of Victor Willis The Village People said in a statement that Willis had died of a "short but aggressive illness." It did not provide any further details as to the cause of death. The Village People added that they are requesting privacy at this time. Willis died a day before his 75th birthday. He was discovered via a demo tape, by the production group, Can't Stop Productions. Though Village People are best known as an ensemble group, Willis recorded the first Village People album solo, using professional backing singers. After the first album was released under the name of Village People and began to climb the charts, more members for the group were quickly assembled. ... Willis left the band in 1979, and numerous singers took up the main singer mantle after his departure, as the Village People continued to perform with different line ups. In 2012, Willis won a landmark legal battle over the rights to hit tracks, including "Y.M.C.A." He was first awarded a 33 percent share of the credits, and then this was raised to 50 percent a few years later. After winning the legal battle, Willis rejoined the Village People in 2017, and the band began touring again. After leaving the Village People, Willis struggled with drug abuse and had a number of drug-related run-ins with law enforcement. In 2006, he completed a treatment program at the Betty Ford Center and remained drug-free. In the years since, he has spoken publicly about the dangers of drug abuse. He is survived by his wife, Karen Huff-Willis. How Y.M.C.A Became an Anthem for President Donald Trump The song "Y.M.C.A." had been popular for years, regularly being played at parties and sporting events and taking up an iconic mantle in pop music in part thanks to the dance associated with it, where people spell out the letters of the song's title with their arms. But in recent years, the song has taken on a new meaning after being adopted by Trump. Trump began to use the song at events and rallies in the early 2020s, and to begin with, the relationship between Trump and Willis was acrimonious. Willis’ wife Karen, a lawyer and the manager for the group, sent Trump’s legal team a cease-and-desist letter after he was filmed dancing to the Village People hit "Macho Man" at Mar-a-Lago. In December 2024, after Trump was re-elected, Willis wrote in a post to Facebook: "Since 2020, I’ve received over a thousand complaints about President Elect Trump’s use of Y.M.C.A. With that many complaints, I decided to ask the President Elect to stop using Y.M.C.A. because his use had become a nuisance to me." However, his feelings about the usage had changed. "I said to my wife one day, hey, "Trump" seems to genuinely like Y.M.C.A. and he’s having a lot of fun with it." He added that Trump’s continued use of the song has seen "financial benefits," with Y.M.C.A. grossing "several million dollars," since he adopted it. "I’m glad I allowed the President Elect’s continued use of Y.M.C.A. And I thank him for choosing to use my song," he said. In his tribute to Willis, Trump spoke about Y.M.C.A. being played at rallies, saying: "Many singers and groups wanted to get on board at the Rallies after all of the Rally Attendance Records were set - The crowds were, and are, enormous - But Victor and the group was there for us right from the beginning! They loved the action, and we loved them and their great and uplifting song." However, there was one point that Willis looked to clarify. The song has long been considered a gay anthem, with the president himself describing it as such. Willis, though, publicly disagreed. "The song is not really a gay anthem other than certain people falsely suggesting that it is. And this must stop because it is damaging to the song," he wrote.
